title: WSL2 Mirrored Mode doesn't Share Ports Nicely
body:
### Issue Description

Running containers on a different IP address from my workstations NIC IP address on a podman ipvlan network I am unable to bind them to the same port as a application running on my workstation. I have verified I can have several applications binding to the same port in Windows 11 and they can share without issue.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Setup WSL2 mirrored networking
2. Create an ipvlan podman network on an interface
3. Run a windows app that binds to that port.
4. Create a container that uses the ipvlan network and tries to bind to the same port.


### Describe the results you received

> Error: cannot listen on the UDP port: listen udp4 :XXXXX: bind: address already in use

### Describe the results you expected

Success and both applications can use the same UDP port on different IP addresses.

### Additional information

UDP sockets. TCP sockets haven't been tried

comments.body: ## I also faced this problem. - wsl2 ![image](https://github.com/containers/podman/assets/11551959/238cf2e8-6bd0-409e-9458-a89a7bff9c74) - win11

@Tonyangl I don't believe your problem is the same as the one in this thread as you do not have a server open on port 9000 running natively on windows. Furthermore you're using ssh to try to connect to an HTTP server

Please provide actual commands what you are doing in your reproducer.

A port can only be bound once for obvious reasons unless something like SO_REUSEPORT is sued but that generally makes no sense for most applications.

Thank you @Luap99 for looking into this.
I am using an ipvlan network and am looking to publish them to the same port but with the unique IP address from the ipvlan (I would be using macvlans if they worked with Windows 11 mirrored networking).

I am using SO_REUSEPORT in both the application running natively on windows as well as the application running within the container. The application in the container has a unique IP address to the host so I should be able to publish the ports of the container to be accessible on the LAN.

```
podman run -d --ip 192.168.1.123 -p 4000:4000/udp container-name
```

I'll take another look in case there's something else I'm missing about how the port binding is working on both sides. I assume with wanting broadcast reception to work that's going to be a different story.

`-p 4000:4000/udp` that tell podman to bind port 4000 on every ip (on the WSL instance), podman doesn't use SO_REUSEPORT so it fails.

In general as root and with ipvlan no port binding can work as root only uses firewall rules to configure that and this cannot be done with macvlan/ipvlan
So to fix your problem just remove the -p from your podman command

I guess the problem then is that I have a container that can communicate out but it seemingly can't receive any messages externally and for my application I need to receive unicast and broadcast packets.

Publishing the ports seems to get that to work but removes my ability to have the windows application running at the same time on the same port. But without it it's unable to receive broadcasts which seem to also be triggering the ability for the port to receive externally initiated unicast traffic.
